truth.papers.A14.title: Strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels
truth.papers.A14.paper_scope: To investigate the strengthening mechanisms (dislocation, grain size, ordered phase, solid solution, lattice friction) in Fe-Al based low-density steels with 5-9 wt% Al, with detailed characterization of nano-sized Fe3Al ordered phase and dislocation density evolution, and to deconstruct the yield strength of the Fe-6.8Al alloy into mechanistic contributions.

prediction.papers.A14.title: Strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels
prediction.papers.A14.paper_scope: Investigation of strengthening mechanisms in Fe-Al based ferritic low-density steels, focusing on Fe-6.8Al, through microstructural characterization (Mössbauer, SAXS, TEM, XRD) and mechanical testing to quantify contributions from grain size, dislocations, ordered phases, and solid solution.

prediction.alloys.alloy_068al.alloys_notes: Primary alloy studied for strengthening mechanisms; density reduction and single-phase ferrite stabilization reported.

truth.processes.proc_05al_powder_anneal.descri: Melted, powdered by filing, vacuum sealed in glass tube and annealed at 600°C for 60 min to relieve internal stress.
truth.processes.proc_06al_powder_anneal.descri: Melted, powdered by filing, vacuum sealed in glass tube and annealed at 600°C for 60 min to relieve internal stress.

truth.processes.proc_09al_powder_anneal.descri: Melted, powdered by filing, vacuum sealed in glass tube and annealed at 600°C for 60 min to relieve internal stress.

truth.structures.sample_05al_powder.overall_st: Single-phase ferrite (BCC) matrix with a small amount of nano-sized D03 ordered Fe3Al phase.
truth.structures.sample_06al_powder.overall_st: Single-phase ferrite matrix with nano-sized D03 ordered Fe3Al phase.
truth.structures.sample_068al_powder.overall_s: Single-phase ferrite with increasing volume fraction of nano Fe3Al D03 ordered phase.
truth.structures.sample_068al_powder.microstru: log-normal, volume fraction and size increase with Al content
truth.structures.sample_09al_powder.overall_st: Single-phase ferrite with the highest volume fraction of nano Fe3Al D03 ordered phase among studied alloys.
truth.structures.sample_068al_cr98.overall_str: Heavily cold-rolled single-phase ferrite with high dislocation density; elongated grains with shear bands; presence of nano Fe3Al ordered phase (expected but not specifically characterized in this state).
truth.structures.sample_068al_cr98.microstruct: heavily elongated pancaked grains with shear bands at ~40° to RD; average lamellae width 2.6 ± 1.2 µm
XRD crystallite size; actual deformed grain size larger (µm scale)
truth.structures.sample_068al_anneal_5min.over: Recrystallized equiaxed ferrite grains with nano Fe3Al ordered phase (DO3) distributed in matrix.
truth.structures.sample_068al_anneal_5min.micr: small (determined by SAXS on powder of same composition; TEM shows fine dispersion)
nano-sized spherical particles (bright in dark-field TEM)
uniformly distributed in matrix (TEM dark field)
truth.structures.sample_068al_anneal_60min.ove: Fully recrystallized equiaxed ferrite with grain growth; nano Fe3Al ordered phase present; low dislocation density.
truth.structures.sample_068al_anneal_60min.mic: small (similar to 5 min condition, but may be coarser)

prediction.structures.sample_05al_powder.overall_st: Ferrite matrix with nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phase
prediction.structures.sample_06al_powder.overall_st: Ferrite matrix with nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phase
prediction.structures.sample_068al_cr98.overall_str: Heavily elongated and pancaked ferrite grains along rolling direction with shear bands at ~40° to RD; disordered ferrite matrix with very high dislocation density
prediction.structures.sample_068al_anneal_5min.over: Equiaxed fully recrystallised ferrite grains with nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) precipitates
prediction.structures.sample_068al_anneal_60min.ove: Equiaxed recrystallised ferrite grains with grain growth and nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) precipitates
prediction.structures.sample_068al_powder.overall_s: Ferrite matrix with nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phase
prediction.structures.sample_09al_powder.overall_st: Ferrite matrix with nano-sized ordered Fe3Al (DO3) phase

Engineering stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 8a

prediction.properties.sample_068al_anneal_60min.mec: Engineering stress-strain curve shown in Fig. 8a

truth.performance.sample_068al_anneal_5min.com: YS 700 vs 342 MPa (~104% higher) attributed to finer grain size (25.2 µm vs 69.2 µm) and additional strengthening mechanisms

truth.characterization_methods.sample_05al_pow: Determine size, volume fraction and number density of nano-sized ordered Fe3Al phase
Ordered phase diameter 2.5 ± 0.15 nm; SAXS volume fractions lower than Mössbauer because it primarily senses phases with sharp boundaries.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_06al_pow: Measure ordered phase size and volume fraction
Diameter ~2.8 nm; intensity increases with Al, indicating larger volume fraction.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_068al_po: Quantify ordered phase characteristics for Fe-6.8Al
Diameter ~3.0 nm (intermediate); Guinier region shifts to lower q with Al indicating size increase.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_09al_pow: Diameter 3.75 ± 0.2 nm; volume fraction highest among studied alloys.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_068al_cr: Measure yield strength, UTS, uniform elongation
YS=1800 MPa, UTS not obtained (failure shortly after yielding), uniform elongation 1.2%.
truth.characterization_methods.sample_068al_an: YS=700 MPa, UTS=870 MPa, uniform elongation 20.0%, yield ratio 0.85.
YS=265 MPa, UTS=394 MPa, uniform elongation 29.8%, yield ratio 0.67.
truth.characterization_methods.idx_15.purpose: Verify chemical composition of alloys after heat treatment
truth.characterization_methods.idx_15.key_find: Compositions confirmed [15]; results reported in Ref. [15].

prediction.characterization_methods.sample_05al_pow: Quantify size, volume fraction and number density of ordered DO3 phase
Radially symmetric 2D profile; weak peak between q = 0.3-3 nm^-1 indicating nano-sized ordered phase scattering.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_06al_pow: Quantify size, volume fraction and number density of ordered DO3 phase
Scattering intensity higher than Fe-5Al; Guinier region shifts towards lower q confirming larger ordered phase size.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_068al_po: Quantify size, volume fraction and number density of ordered DO3 phase
Scattering intensity increased relative to lower-Al alloys; fitted with log-normal size distribution model assuming spherical DO3 particles.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_09al_pow: Quantify size, volume fraction and number density of ordered DO3 phase
Highest scattering intensity among studied alloys; largest Guinier region shift confirming the largest ordered phase size and volume fraction.
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_068al_cr: Electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) with wavelength dispersive spectroscopy (WDS)
Final compositions examined at 15 kV accelerating voltage; reported in Ref. [15].
prediction.characterization_methods.sample_068al_an: Yield strength = 700 MPa, UTS = 870 MPa, uniform elongation = 20.0%, yield ratio = 0.85; gauge parallel to rolling direction; strain rate 10^-3 s^-1.
Yield strength = 265 MPa, UTS = 394 MPa, uniform elongation = 29.8%, yield ratio = 0.67; gauge parallel to rolling direction; strain rate 10^-3 s^-1.

truth.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Relate ΔBhf to local atomic configuration (Eq. 1: B = B0 - n1ΔB1 - n2ΔB2)
Used to deconvolute Mössbauer spectra and assign peaks to Fe-Al atomic arrangements and ordered phases.
truth.computational_details.idx_1.analytical_m: Local mono-disperse model with log-normal distribution
Fit SAXS intensity to extract size, volume fraction and number density of ordered phase (Eqs. 2–5)
Δρ (scattering contrast), dN number density, rp peak radius, w standard deviation; form factor for spheres
Used to determine ordered phase diameter from 2.5 to 3.75 nm across alloys.
X-Pert PRO, MAUD (Rietveld), ANIZC (dislocation contrast factor)
Solid solution strengthening (Fleischer)
τss = (G εs^(3/2) c^(1/2)) / 700 (Eq. 9); converted to σ using Taylor factor.
G=82 GPa, c=6.8 wt% Al, εs=0.45, τss=95.6 MPa for Fe-6.8Al
Contribution constant across conditions.
Predicted contributions summed and compared with measured yield strength (Fig. 9)
Lattice frictional stress (Peierls-Nabarro)
τPN = (2G/(1-ν)) exp(-2πa/(b(1-ν))) (Eq. 10); room temperature value 41 MPa used
G=82 GPa, ν=0.33, a=2.02 Å, b=2.48 Å; τPN at 0 K ~154.34 MPa; adopted RT value 41 MPa from literature for steel
Provided constant base stress.
Order strengthening
γ antiphase boundary energy, f, r ordered phase fraction and radius (from SAXS/TEM)
Contribution increased with ordered phase size and fraction.
Hall-Petch grain boundary strengthening
ΔσGB = kHP D^(-1/2) (Eq. 12)
kHP for pure iron; D from SEM/XRD
Dominant in fine-grained state.
Taylor dislocation strengthening
Δσρ = M α G b ρ^(1/2) (Eq. 13)
M Taylor factor, α=0.33, G=82 GPa, b Burgers vector, ρ from XRD variance method
Major contribution in cold-rolled state.

prediction.computational_details.idx_0.software_use: NORMOS (Mössbauer fitting), FIT2D (SAXS integration), MAUD (Rietveld refinement), ANIZC (dislocation contrast factor calculation)
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.validation_a: Analytical strengthening model predictions compared against measured yield strengths of Fe-6.8Al in 98% CR, 750 °C/5 min and 750 °C/60 min conditions.
prediction.computational_details.idx_0.analytical_m: Applied using Hall-Petch constant for 99.95% pure iron from literature; grain sizes from SEM and XRD.
M, α = 0.33, G = 82 GPa, b, ρ (dislocation density)
Dislocation density determined by XRD variance method on (110) peak profile.
Calculated τ_ss = 95.6 MPa for Fe-6.8Al; contribution increases from 67.8 MPa (5 wt% Al) to 127.2 MPa (9 wt% Al).
Calculated τ_PN ~154.34 MPa at 0 K; room temperature value taken as 41 MPa from Speich and Swann for 0.4 wt% C-steel due to lack of Fe-Al data.
G = 82 GPa, γ (antiphase boundary energy), b, f (volume fraction), r (radius)
Applied for nano-sized Fe3Al (DO3) ordered phase using size and volume fraction from TEM and SAXS.

truth.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Addition of Al up to ~10 wt% leads to density reduction of up to 8% and formation of ferrite (BCC) phase.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: Young's modulus reduction upon Al addition was reported by Rana et al., limiting automotive use, but is not measured in this study.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Yield ratio (YS/UTS) calculated: 98%CR not available, 5 min anneal 0.85, 60 min anneal 0.67.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: Strain hardening rate curves (Fig. 8b) shown for 5 and 60 min annealed samples; higher rate for 60 min.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: The ordered phase identified as Fe3Al with D03 structure; a possible B2 (FeAl) phase was indicated by low-intensity peak at ~23 T in Mössbauer spectra for Al ≥ 6.8%, but volume fraction low.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Lattice frictional stress for Fe-Al at room temperature taken as 41 MPa from literature for 0.4 wt% C steel due to unavailability of data.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Comparison with dual-phase Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels: the single-phase Fe-6.8Al after 5 min anneal (YS=700 MPa, elongation 20%) rivals properties of lean alloyed duplex steels (YS=472 MPa, UTS=635 MPa, elongation 19.7% from Rana et al. [8,9]).
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: Shear bands observed at ~40° to RD in 98% CR sample; these indicate non-uniform deformation.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_8.value: 98% CR sample did not show measurable UTS because failure occurred soon after yielding.
truth.unmapped_findings.idx_9.value: SAXS intensity at high q (>3 nm^-1) showed incoherent flat background; at low q (<0.3 nm^-1) intensity decays as q^-4, attributed to larger heterogeneities (grains/grain boundaries).

prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_0.value: Fig. 4 shows SAXS-based diameter and volume fraction of Fe3Al ordered phase as: Fe-5Al (~2.6 nm, 0.4%), Fe-6Al (~2.8 nm, 0.7%), Fe-6.8Al (~2.7 nm, 0.8% as 7 wt% Al in figure table), and Fe-9Al (~3.7 nm, 1.9%); the text quotes a general increase from 2.5 ± 0.15 nm to 3.75 ± 0.2 nm with increasing Al content.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_1.value: TEM investigation of Fe-6.8Al detected ordered phase size of 2.1 ± 0.2 nm, but the specific annealing condition (5 min or 60 min) of the TEM sample was not explicitly stated in the text; it was mapped to the 5 min sample as the primary studied condition.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_2.value: Dislocation density for the 98% CR sample was reported as ~2.2 × 10^15 m^-2 and for the 750 °C/60 min sample as ~10^12 m^-2; the exact value for the 750 °C/5 min sample was not explicitly stated but was described to show a three-order drop in magnitude upon annealing.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_3.value: The yield strength of Fe-6.8Al annealed at 750 °C for 5 min (700 MPa) is significantly higher than the 342 MPa reported by Rana et al. [3] for annealing at 900 °C for 1 min, attributed to the smaller ferrite grain size (25.2 ± 5.0 μm vs 69.2 ± 34.8 μm).
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_4.value: Engineering stress-strain curves and strain hardening rate curves are shown in Fig. 8, but specific numerical values beyond those in Table 4 were not provided in the text; the strain hardening rate for the 60 min annealed sample was qualitatively described as higher than the 5 min sample initially, with a sudden increase followed by gradual decrease.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_5.value: Mössbauer spectroscopy indicated that the volume fractions of ordered phase observed by SAXS are lower than those of the DO3 phase observed by Mössbauer spectroscopy because SAXS probes ordered regions with sharper boundaries whereas Mössbauer traces all local environments including diffused boundaries.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_6.value: Density measurements were undertaken by Archimedes principle as reported in Ref. [15], but no numerical density values were provided in the present text.
prediction.unmapped_findings.idx_7.value: The paper mentions that for very fine ordered domains, solid solution strengthening contribution is difficult to separate from ordered phase strengthening and was therefore ignored in such cases.
